title: Chapter 16: Siege Mentality
content: Derek Voss adjusted to our supremacy of the upper floors by going to ground.

For three days after the stairwell confrontation, Mia's surveillance reported the same pattern: Derek left 8A only to raid the vending machines on Floors 7 and 6, always with Rick in tow, always with the pistol visible on his hip. Maria stayed behind, presumably under orders—and possibly under lock and key. He was fortifying, hoarding, and waiting.

"Classic siege psychology," Mia reported from the roof, her voice crackling through the two-way radio I'd scored from a system daily quest. "He's got enough food for two weeks if he rations. He's got three people—four if you count Maria as an asset, which he does. He thinks time is on his side because he figures we'll run out of supplies first."
"Will we?"
"I've been watching your system rewards. You're pulling in more calories per day than a family of four. Whatever that watch is, it's not stingy."

She wasn't wrong. The daily quests had become almost routine: feed Luna, receive food. Groom Luna, receive supplies. Spend quality time with Luna, receive upgrades. The system was a slot machine that always paid out, and the currency was emotional authenticity.

But supplies were only part of the equation. We had food, water, and shelter. What we didn't have was *space*—Apartment 12B was designed for one person, and we were now four humans, one recovering zombie, and a cat. The tension was building in ways that had nothing to do with Derek.
The first fight happened on Day Thirteen.

It started with the bathroom. One bathroom, five people, zero privacy. Jessica had been in there for forty-five minutes—she showered obsessively, a trauma response that Sarah had quietly explained to me—and Luna had reached her limit.
"In," Luna said, standing outside the bathroom door with the particular intensity that meant she wasn't asking.
"One minute!" Jessica's voice, small and guilty.
"Now."
The door opened. Jessica slipped out, wrapped in a towel, her wet hair plastered to her face, and Luna went in without acknowledging her. Jessica's expression crumpled.
"She doesn't like me," Jessica said to no one in particular.

"She doesn't know you yet," Sarah said, appearing from the kitchen with a cup of water—the rationing was strict, but Sarah had a gift for finding extra portions for people who needed them. "Give her time."
"She's a zombie. How much time does she need?"

Six words too many. Luna came out of the bathroom, her face blank with that particular zombie stillness that meant she'd heard everything, and she looked at Jessica with an expression I couldn't read—hurt, maybe, or the bone-deep weariness of someone who'd been dead and was tired of being reminded of it.
"I am Luna," she said. Precise. Controlled. "I was dead. Now I am not. I am learning. I am slow. But I am *here*. Where were you?"
She went to the bedroom and closed the door.
Jessica burst into tears.
I intervened. Badly.
"Hey, she didn't mean—"

"She's right!" Jessica sobbed. "I've been here three days and I just... I can't. Everything is wrong. The world is dead, there's a zombie in the next room, some guy wants to kill us, and I can't even brush my teeth without someone waiting outside the door!"
The crying escalated. Sarah shot me a look that clearly said *you're making it worse* and took over, guiding Jessica to the couch with practiced hands.
I retreated to the bedroom. Luna was sitting on the bed, very still, her hands folded in her lap. She didn't look at me when I sat beside her.
"She called me zombie," Luna said.
"You are—"
"I am Luna." The words came out hard, sharp, with a force I'd never heard from her before. "Not zombie. Not dead thing. Not monster. Luna. I chose this name. I am *becoming*."

Her fist clenched, and the bedside table cracked—a hairline fracture that spoke to the depth of her emotion. Luna, who could crush steel, was trying so hard to be gentle that the effort was literally breaking the furniture.
"You're right," I said. "I'm sorry I didn't correct her."
"Will she learn?"
"If you give her a chance, yeah. She's scared, Luna. She doesn't understand what you are—none of us do, really. But she'll figure it out. She just needs to see what I see."
"What do you see?"
I looked at her—really looked, the way I had on that first day in the hallway, when I'd seen a zombie girl and said *you're beautiful* and meant it.

"I see someone who's fighting her way back from nothing," I said. "Someone who chose 'stay' as her first word because she didn't want to be alone. Someone who carries a fire axe and protects people she's never met. Someone who's learning to laugh, and talk, and hug, and be jealous, and every single one of those things is a miracle."

My hand found hers. Her grip was careful, always careful. But this time there was something else in it—gratitude, maybe, or the fierce love of someone who'd been told she was a monster and didn't want to be.
"You see me," she said quietly. "Not the dead thing. *Me.*"
"I see you, Luna."
She leaned into me, her forehead against my shoulder, and stayed there for a long time.
**AFFECTION UPDATE: LUNA — 52%**
Over halfway. I hadn't even noticed the number climbing—it had happened on its own, through feeding and caring and holding hands and saying her name like it meant something.
Because it did.
That evening, I called a house meeting.

"New rules," I said, standing in the living room with everyone assembled—even Mia, who'd come down from the roof for the occasion. "First: no one calls Luna a zombie. She's Luna. Period. Second: bathroom schedule. One hour per person per day, max. Third: everyone does daily quests with me."
"Daily quests?" Mia asked, skeptical.

"The system rewards daily interactions. Feeding Luna, group meals, shared activities. The more we engage as a group, the more supplies we get. It's not about romance—it's about connection. Genuine connection."
"And if we fake it?"
"Hollow Heart penalty. Rewards revoked. Trust me, the system knows."

Jessica raised her hand, still red-eyed from crying. "I'm sorry," she said, directing it at the bedroom door, which opened a crack to reveal Luna's face. "I was scared and I said something cruel. You're not a zombie. You're Luna. And I'm grateful you got me out of there."
The door opened wider. Luna stepped out, looked at Jessica, and offered her hand.
Jessica took it.
The system pulsed:

**GROUP DYNAMIC IMPROVED**
**DAILY QUEST BONUS: +20% reward value for next 24 hours**
Small steps. But steps.

The apartment was still too small, the world was still too broken, and Derek Voss was still out there, waiting for our guard to drop. But for one evening, sitting around a kitchen table with crackers and peanut butter and a cat who demanded tributes of tuna, we weren't survivors.
We were a family.
Strange, mismatched, impossible, and held together by a magic watch and sheer stubbornness.
But a family nonetheless.
